result.chapter_summary: The author draws a parallel between the educational decline in France following its 1989 reforms and the decline in the United States starting in the 1960s, arguing both were caused by the adoption of skill-based, individualized progressive education. He contends that the decline is evidenced by a massive drop in standardized test scores across all demographics, which cannot be explained away by changes in student populations.

claims.text: The educational decline in French elementary schools since 1989 is a 'débâcle' comparable to the French military defeat of 1940.
claims.source_passage: To speak of 'The Educational Fall of France' seems at first rhetorical overkill-equating mere lower scores in French elementary schools with the military defeat of France in World War II. But it isn't just an over-the-top play on words. It was the explicit theme of an important 2007 book edited by Laurent Lafforgue and Liliane Lurçat: La Débâcle de l'École: Une Tragédie Incomprise.

claims.text: French intellectuals made significant and avoidable intellectual mistakes in 1989 that damaged the school system.
claims.source_passage: His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989, just as higher-ups had made serious, avoidable military mistakes in 1940.

claims.text: France's educational system declined from being one of the best and most equitable in the world to one of the worst and least equitable after adopting skill-based principles.
claims.source_passage: The data showed another educational system that, like our own, had adopted skill-based, individualized, educational principles, and subsequently declined from being among the best and most equitable large school systems in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable.

claims.text: The decline of student verbal achievement in France closely mirrors the fall of verbal scores in the United States during the 1960s and 1970s.
claims.source_passage: I reached that conclusion when I found that the decline of student verbal achievement in France closely mirrored the infamous fall of verbal scores in the United States in the 1960s and 1970s.

claims.text: The 1930s progressive curriculum reforms in the US were the primary cause of the massive score declines in the 1960s and 1970s.
claims.source_passage: If we insist that the nationwide progressive 'reforms' of the curriculum in the 1930s and 1940s did not chiefly cause that disaster, we simply protect the faulty ideas that are the key to the whole conundrum.

claims.text: SAT verbal scores plummeted by half a standard deviation between 1960 and 1980.
claims.source_passage: SAT verbal scores had plummeted half a standard deviation in the twenty years between 1960 and 1980.

claims.text: The 1965-1980 test score decline occurred across all US school tests and affected students of all income levels.
claims.source_passage: Scholars have now shown that there was a significant score drop on virtually all US school tests- not just on the SAT-by all students, rich and poor, during the period 1965-1980.

claims.text: The flat verbal scores of US seventeen-year-olds since 1971 indicate that education has failed to recover from the initial decline.
claims.source_passage: According to the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) the verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed flat ever since 1971, the year we first began national sampling, a decade after our decline was well under way.

claims.text: Stable demographics in Iowa and Minnesota during their score drops eliminate demographic change as a causal factor for the decline.
claims.source_passage: Particularly persuasive are the state tests in Iowa and Minnesota, administered to virtually all high school juniors and seniors in those states at a time when their pupil demographics were stable-thus eliminating demographic change as a cause of the declines in those states.

claims.text: American schools are infected by a system of ideas called 'providential individualism' that prevents educational improvement.
claims.source_passage: Our schools have long been infected by a system of ideas that prevents improvement from taking hold... Like a virus, providential individualism knows no national boundary or political party.

claims.text: Germany was the nineteenth-century nursery of progressive education, which later influenced the United States.
claims.source_passage: Germany, which was the nineteenth-century nursery of progressive education, and has had a decisive influence on educational thought and policy in the United States.

claims.text: The educational ideas that prevent improvement in American schools are like a virus that knows no national or political boundaries.
claims.source_passage: Like a virus, providential individualism knows no national boundary or political party.

claims.text: The cause and cure for the educational decline seen in the United States have been definitively determined by the recent history of the French school system.
claims.source_passage: The cause of the disease has now been definitively determined in Paris and the cure as well. We should not hesitate to import that knowledge.

claims.text: France has completed an unintended natural experiment over twenty years that provides high-quality statistical evidence regarding educational outcomes.
claims.source_passage: France, with its excellent record keeping, has in effect recently completed an unintended natural experiment with ten-year-old students over a period of twenty years. The statistical and sociological analysis is on a par with our research gold standard—the National Assessment of Educational Progress.

claims.text: American ten-year-olds are not fundamentally different from French ten-year-olds regarding their potential for math and reading proficiency.
claims.source_passage: We are not so imbued with American exceptionalism as to believe that American ten-year-olds are fundamentally different from French ones with respect to math and reading proficiency.

claims.text: In the 1980s, France and Sweden possessed the best large school systems in Europe in terms of achievement and equity.
claims.source_passage: In the 1980s, France could celebrate (with Sweden) the best large school systems in Europe.

claims.text: A grade-by-grade specific curriculum induces publishers to produce more effective and attractive learning materials at a competitive price.
claims.source_passage: The advantages of definite grade-by-grade topics for inducing publishers to produce excellent learning materials were clear. The publishers had no control over the basic content of schooling; they gained their competitive advantage by presenting the content attractively and effectively at a good price.

claims.text: Pre-1989 French schooling educated immigrants more effectively than any other European nation.
claims.source_passage: France exhibited the highest average student achievement with the greatest equity of any large, diverse country. It educated immigrants better than any other European nation.

claims.text: France's success in overcoming inequality prior to 1989 was due to its excellent preschool system and its highly communal primary schools with a common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: I had long attributed this earlier French accomplishment in overcoming inequality to the excellent French preschool system being attended by nearly 100 percent of four- and five-year-olds, and also to the fact that after preschool, children entered a highly communal primary school with a well-developed common curriculum.

claims.text: The intellectual preparation for the 1989 French educational reform had been developing in French education schools since the 1960s.
claims.source_passage: the intellectual preparation for the catastrophic loi Jospin of 1989 had been brewing in French education schools since the 1960s.

claims.text: The 1989 loi Jospin overthrew the French educational tradition of using a common curriculum to unify society and reduce inequality.
claims.source_passage: But just twelve years later, in 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school. Instead of acquiring the same culture, each school was to respect and develop the child's home culture

claims.text: The centralization of the French system allows for more confident inferences about the effects of curricular change than is possible in the decentralized United States.
claims.source_passage: The suddenness and universality of its curricular changes enable us now to make some confident inferences of the sort that are impossible in the United States, where specific curricular content over time is largely unspecified and unknown.

claims.text: Germany performed poorly in student achievement in the early 1990s because it remained under the influence of progressive educational ideas.
claims.source_passage: But note also the poor performance of Germany, still under the sway of progressive ideas.

claims.text: The basic idea of French schooling in 1977 was to enable all children to acquire the same knowledge to unify society and reduce inequality.
claims.source_passage: who stated the basic idea of French schooling: to enable all French children to acquire the same knowledge in order to unify society and reduce inequality.

claims.text: In 1989, France completely overthrew its tradition of uniform national education by passing a new law.
claims.source_passage: But just twelve years later, in 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school.

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reform mandated that schools respect and develop a child's home culture rather than a shared national culture.
claims.source_passage: Instead of acquiring the same culture, each school was to respect and develop the child's home culture and conform to the individual characteristics of each locality and to the individual characteristics of each child.

claims.text: The 1989 radical change in French education was structurally based on the Bourdieu-Gros Report.
claims.source_passage: The structure of the radical change was to be based on a new report called the Bourdieu-Gros Report.

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu argued that the old national curriculum sustained the culture of the well-off and excluded children from other backgrounds.
claims.source_passage: Rather, he said, it sustained a culture that the well-off already possessed, and thus it excluded children who came from other cultures.

claims.text: The Loi Jospin (1989) decentralized French education by dividing metropolitan France into twenty-six autonomous school districts.
claims.source_passage: Structurally, a radical feature of the new law was decentralization. Instead of having everything emanate from Paris, metropolitan France would henceforth be divided into twenty-six autonomous school districts.

claims.text: The new French education colleges were established to train teachers in progressive educational theory under the guise of 'pedagogy'.
claims.source_passage: They were to codify the profession of pedagogy in a scientific and orderly way (translation: they would teach progressive theory).

claims.text: The 1989 French national standards (the 'socle') were too short and nonspecific to be practically useful.
claims.source_passage: the standards document was astonishingly short and nonspecific—nonexistent in practical terms.

claims.text: The Jospin law prioritized 'all-purpose skills' over the accumulation of factual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The emphasis was to be on skills, not facts, based on the statement in the Bourdieu-Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.

claims.text: The preamble of the 1989 French law established child-centeredness as the guiding principle of education.
claims.source_passage: The very preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be "lockstep" any longer. It was going to be child centered.

claims.text: The 1989 law allowed French students to elaborate their own curricula based on their personal aspirations and capacities.
claims.source_passage: Pupils and students will elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.

claims.text: The French Ministry of Education continues to instruct schools to prioritize individual student diversity despite years of educational decline.
claims.source_passage: The schools were commanded to adopt individualism by taking "into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil" (an instruction the French Ministry of Education still gives to schools today, even after many years of decline).

claims.text: Progressive education ('la nouvelle éducation') took root in France because teachers were indoctrinated with these ideas in training colleges during the 1960s.
claims.source_passage: The first impulse was felt by new teachers trained in the ideas of the 1960s. They had been instructed... in a new point of view—that of la nouvelle éducation, which we call "progressive education."

claims.text: Progressive education de-emphasizes memorization and grammar in favor of naturalistic teaching of math and reading.
claims.source_passage: It encouraged the naturalistic teaching of math and reading. It de-emphasized memorization and grammar.

claims.text: Two main intellectual impulses led French educators to accept and demand a radical change in the school curriculum between 1977 and 1989.
claims.source_passage: There were two main intellectual impulses that led educators to accept and even demand a radical change in the school curriculum.

claims.text: New French teachers trained in the 1960s were instructed in 'la nouvelle education' (progressive education) at teacher-training institutes (écoles normales).
claims.source_passage: They had been instructed, during their training at the écoles normales, in a new point of view-that of la nouvelle education, which we call "progressive education."

claims.text: Progressive education prioritizes the individual child and encourages teachers to accommodate teaching to the specific characteristics of each child.
claims.source_passage: It placed the individual child at the center of concern, and encouraged the teacher to accommodate teaching to the characteristics of the child.

claims.text: While national curriculum topics remained set, the progressive orientation changed the methodology of how reading, writing, and arithmetic were taught.
claims.source_passage: This new orientation could not change the topics of instruction since these were set forth in the national curriculum. But the new attitude did change how reading, writing, and arithmetic were taught.

claims.text: The increase in French primary students failing to read and having to repeat grades was a consequence of new progressive teaching methods, not the old curriculum.
claims.source_passage: It had not been understood that the rise in the number of students who were unable to read and reckon was a consequence of the new methods, not the old ones!

claims.text: The intellectual origins of the French educational decline are the same as those that caused the American educational crisis: naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism.
claims.source_passage: Thus, the intellectual origins of the French 'debacle' were largely the same theories as the ones that created 'a nation at risk' in the United States: the theory that schooling should be natural, and that the aim of schooling should be the development of individuality and critical-thinking skills.

claims.text: Naturalistic reading theories like 'la méthode globale' and 'la méthode mixte' caused basic reading difficulties in young French pupils.
claims.source_passage: The new naturalistic theories into which French teachers were being indoctrinated-for example, in la méthode globale, and la méthode mixte in reading-had begun to cause basic reading difficulties among the youngest pupils

claims.text: The rise in grade retentions caused by progressive methods was used as a rhetorical weapon against the old curriculum and national uniformity.
claims.source_passage: The rise in grade retentions, caused by the new methods, was then deployed successfully as a further argument against the old pedagogy and the old uniformity of the topics in the national curriculum.

claims.text: The 1968 student revolts in France criticized all established institutions, creating a demand for radical curricular change.
claims.source_passage: But there was also a second, different criticism of the old curriculum and its pedagogy that gave high intellectual respectability and urgency to the demand for radical curricular change-a demand that had continued to animate French politics ever since the alarming student revolts of May 1968.

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu's 'The Inheritors' argued that French republican education was a mechanism for reproducing social class rather than overcoming it.
claims.source_passage: His radical claim: French schooling was a means of reproducing social class, not overcoming it.

claims.text: Bourdieu's claim that equality of opportunity in the French system was a myth was based on class-based statistical disparities in university majors and cultural tastes.
claims.source_passage: In this influential book Bourdieu and his coauthor Jean-Claude Passeron claimed that French schooling was not as egalitarian as it conceived itself to be. . . By a clear statistical margin these decisions about a major study area were class based.

claims.text: Bourdieu’s analysis in 'The Inheritors' is superficial because it only examines a single generation's time slice.
claims.source_passage: To be an American reading The Inheritors is to be astonished at its superficiality, its indignant making of mountains out of molehills. The book embraces far too narrow a time slice in describing the social-class-based preferences of university students-merely a single generation.

claims.text: The presence of peasant children in universities proves that the French republican school system of the 1950s and 60s was effectively facilitating social mobility.
claims.source_passage: What The Inheritors showed unwittingly was that the French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s had been doing an excellent job of bringing children of peasants to the university, thus enabling Bourdieu to study their attitudes.

claims.text: The success of Pierre Bourdieu himself contradicts his theory of social reproduction.
claims.source_passage: Bourdieu himself had grown up under disadvantage; Standard French was not his home language. No mere social reproduction there!

claims.text: The French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s was highly effective at enabling social mobility for children from disadvantaged backgrounds.
claims.source_passage: What The Inheritors showed unwittingly was that the French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s had been doing an excellent job of bringing children of peasants to the university

claims.text: The old French centralized common curriculum was a product of egalitarian left-wing politics rather than right-wing conservatism.
claims.source_passage: The old French centralized system with a common curriculum was not, as any on the French left imagined, a product of hidebound right-wing politics, but of the old egalitarian left

claims.text: The Bourdieu-Gros Report was fatally vague regarding the specific content of the curriculum.
claims.source_passage: to read the Bourdieu-Gros Report, which became the basis for new 1989 law, is to be struck by its vagueness regarding the curriculum.

claims.text: The growth of knowledge does not render a broad, factual curriculum (encyclopedism) vain or obsolete.
claims.source_passage: It looked with disdain upon mere facts, or 'encylopedism.' It stated: 'The growth of knowledge renders vain the ambition of encyclopedism.' ... Its assumptions are incorrect

claims.text: The 1989 French educational law essentially recommended and implemented an American-style education model.
claims.source_passage: In essence the Bourdieu-Gros Report recommended American-style education, and that is what the new law went on to require.

claims.text: The data collected by the French Ministry of Education constitutes the most definitive comparative study of curriculum effects ever undertaken.
claims.source_passage: The results are decisive, and constitute the most definitive comparative study of curriculum effects ever undertaken in any country.

claims.text: The implementation of a child-centered skills curriculum caused average literacy scores in France to fall by 0.4 standard deviations.
claims.source_passage: The child-centered skills curriculum had caused the average of literacy scores to fall four-tenths of a standard deviation.

claims.text: The decline in educational achievement following the 1989 reform disproportionately affected the neediest students.
claims.source_passage: Among the neediest students, the decline was severe.

claims.text: The differential effect of the decline proves that a common grade-by-grade curriculum has positive equity effects.
claims.source_passage: It suggests the positive equity effects of a common grade-by-grade curriculum in the elementary school when the lines are traced backward in time— what had been lost and what could be regained.

claims.text: Variables including buildings, teachers, classrooms, and budgets remained similar across the decades of the French study, isolating curriculum as the primary variable.
claims.source_passage:
Buildings, teachers, classrooms, and
budgets remained similar through the decades. The demographic
categorizations of "head of household" stayed the same.

claims.text: Educational achievement declines under a skills-based curriculum are more severe for disadvantaged students than for advantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Because children with better-educated parents acquire academic knowledge both at home and at school, the decline was less steep among more- advantaged students. Among the neediest students, the decline was severe.

claims.text: Literacy test performance is directly dependent on a student possessing the knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: If students gain the knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere, they will score well on a literacy test.

claims.text: If schools fail to provide communal knowledge, a child's educational success is determined entirely by 'accidents of birth.'
claims.source_passage: If accidents of birth have excluded that knowledge from the home environment, and if the school does not supply it, then they will score badly on a literacy test.

claims.text: The individualized and localized school arrangements implemented in France in 1990 intensified social reproduction rather than diminishing it.
claims.source_passage: As the French data from 1987 to 2007 show, the new 1990 arrangements greatly intensified the social reproduction they were supposed to diminish.

claims.text: The 1990 reforms systematically deprived poor children of the enabling knowledge that wealthy children acquire at home.
claims.source_passage: They systematically deprived poor children of the enabling knowledge that rich children had acquired from their home environments.

claims.text: Teacher quality in France remained high and constant throughout the period of achievement decline.
claims.source_passage: I would stress that entry standards for teachers into the French school system remained extremely high both before and after Jospin. “Teacher quality” remained constant in France by all objective measures during the period of decline.

claims.text: A specific, cumulative common-core curriculum is superior to an individualized, child-centered curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The French experiment... has pretty well proved that a specific, cumulative common-core curriculum, such as France had before the Jospin law, is far superior to the American-style individualized curriculum that followed it in France.

claims.text: American school districts that implement a communal, cumulative curriculum in all subjects will see higher achievement and equity.
claims.source_passage: These data make it nearly certain that any district in the United States that implemented a communal, cumulative curriculum in all subjects (not just language arts and math) will show higher achievement and much higher equity than it has shown in the past under the reign of the skills delusion.

claims.text: The replacement of common knowledge with individualized curricula in France is an unethical 'natural experiment.'
claims.source_passage: If these trials had occurred in medical research, with epidemiological data from several thousand randomly assigned experimental subjects, they would quickly be halted. It would be considered unethical to continue them.

claims.text: French teacher-training institutes became characterized by boredom, misinformation, and ideological conformity following the Jospin reforms.
claims.source_passage: She was attending one of the new education schools in order to get her license but was so outraged by the boredom, misinformation, and ideological conformity thrust upon her that she resolved to keep a journal.

claims.text: The French educational shift from common knowledge to individualized curricula and skills constitutes a large-scale natural experiment.
claims.source_passage: The French have now conducted a bigger natural experiment, replacing commonality of knowledge with individualized curricula and an emphasis on skills.

claims.text: If the French educational reforms were a medical trial, they would be halted as unethical due to clear negative results.
claims.source_passage: If these trials had occurred in medical research, with epidemiological data from several thousand randomly assigned experimental subjects, they would quickly be halted. It would be considered unethical to continue them.

claims.text: Modern French education colleges redefine teachers as 'resource persons' or 'guides' rather than instructors.
claims.source_passage: Education colleges required a teacher to be called a 'resource person,' a guide on the side. A class lesson was to be considered a 'session,' not a lecture.

claims.text: The chief aim of current French educational theory is the imparting of all-purpose skills like 'learning to learn.'
claims.source_passage: The chief aim was the imparting of all-purpose skills like 'learning to learn.'

claims.text: The 'balanced literacy' or 'méthode mixte' approach is a version of the naturalistic whole-language method that only occasionally includes phonics.
claims.source_passage: They should learn to read by the naturalistic 'whole-language' approach, or (since that is so controversial) by the quasi-whole-language méthode mixte known in English as 'balanced literacy,' which occasionally mixes in some phonics instruction.

claims.text: Progressive, child-centered methods in France have been dominant in education schools since at least the late 1970s.
claims.source_passage: From his book's opening pages he made clear that the methods and attitudes Boutonnet described were already dominant in education schools more than twenty years before.

claims.text: Students taught using traditional methods like phonics and dictation perform better than those taught using progressive 'modern' methods.
claims.source_passage: Yet students of older teachers, who dared to continue to use dictations and learn reading by phonics, fared better.

claims.text: Modern progressive education methods serve as a justification for lowering academic ambitions for children.
claims.source_passage: Modern education only serves to justify the abandonment of ambitions that we have for our children.

claims.text: The disintegration of the primary school curriculum is the primary cause of the French educational crisis.
claims.source_passage: We situate the main origin of the school crisis in the disintegration of the curriculum—principally the primary school curriculum.

claims.text: A disintegrated curriculum makes the subject matter taught essentially random, preventing students from gaining the knowledge necessary for success.
claims.source_passage: this curricular disintegration has rendered more or less random the subject matters taught. It has thus compromised the possibility for the majority of students to gain the wider knowledge that is necessary to academic success.

claims.text: The analysis by French teachers that a national curriculum is a necessary condition for the democratization of education has not been seriously heeded in France.
claims.source_passage: This perceptive analysis by the teachers themselves has not been seriously heeded in France. There has been no successful movement to specify once again the content of the primary curriculum across the nation.

claims.text: As of 2012, France deteriorated more than any other country in the world regarding the equity of its schools according to PISA rankings.
claims.source_passage: the most recent of which, in 2012, showed that France, of all countries in the world, has deteriorated the most in the equity of its schools.

claims.text: Current French legislative adjustments change structural elements but fail to fix the fundamental issues with the elementary curriculum.
claims.source_passage: New adjustments to the loi Jospin have been enacted, which change structural elements at the edges, but do not rationalize the elementary curriculum.

claims.text: Structural adjustments to the loi Jospin have failed to rationalize the French elementary curriculum.
claims.source_passage: New adjustments to the loi Jospin have been enacted, which change structural elements at the edges, but do not rationalize the elementary curriculum.

claims.text: The 2005 French law establishing a 'socle' or base of knowledge has been essentially useless because it did not specify content.
claims.source_passage: A new law in 2005 reconstituted the idea that there should be a more significant base, or socle, that all children should learn, but the resulting document did not get into specifics, and has been essentially useless.

claims.text: A return to a specific national curriculum currently appears unthinkable in the French political context.
claims.source_passage: There is no direct movement to return to a specific national curriculum in the primary school. That seems now as unthinkable in France as it would be in the United States.

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu's ideas regarding educational transmission are outworn and failed.
claims.source_passage: the old ideas-including the outworn and failed ideas of Bourdieu-are still presenting themselves as new.

claims.text: The French educational system has become the most inegalitarian in Europe due to its rejection of knowledge transmission.
claims.source_passage: Fifty years after The Inheritors, our educational system, greatly influenced by that book's condemnation of transmission, has become the most inegalitarian in Europe.

claims.text: The decline of public education in France forces parents to use private tactics and neighborhood selection to ensure their children's success, favoring the wealthy.
claims.source_passage: The only survivors of the shipwreck of our public education are those whose parents know the tactics for escaping the disaster-which quartiers to live in, which subjects to emphasize, which options to choose.

claims.text: The renunciation of teaching a common national culture specifically targets and harms the children of immigrant families.
claims.source_passage: On the other end of the spectrum are the designated victims of social selection-among them, the children of immigrant families. It is with them in mind that we renounced teaching the culture of our country and transmitting to all a common heritage.

claims.text: The denial of cultural transmission in schools will eventually lead to social violence.
claims.source_passage: Who can fail to see that that is the true violence; that such denial of transmission cannot fail, one day, to explode?

claims.text: The political right can also drive decentralization and individualism through free-market ideologies.
claims.source_passage: Bourdieu and the political left cannot boast sole ownership of decentralization, individualism, and the skills delusion. Sweden now offers a similarly compelling example from the right.

claims.text: Educational decentralization in Sweden caused a severe drop in student abilities in core subjects.
claims.source_passage: He showed that decentralization had caused a severe drop in students' abilities in reading, writing, and arithmetic.

claims.text: Swedish decentralization widened achievement gaps between rich and poor students in the same manner as French reforms.
claims.source_passage: It had greatly widened the achievement gaps between rich and poor students. Decentralization of the curriculum had done in Sweden exactly what it had done in France.

claims.text: Prior to 1994, the Swedish school system was one of the most egalitarian in the world with minimal performance gaps based on geography or wealth.
claims.source_passage: Before the changes occurred, Lewin showed, Swedish schools exhibited only minor gaps between rich and poor students, and Sweden boasted one of the most egalitarian systems in the world.

claims.text: The Swedish state abandoned the determination of knowledge content, transferring it to local schools and market forces.
claims.source_passage: the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school, and transferred that determination to local schools.

claims.text: Before 1994, there was little difference in overall school performance in Sweden regardless of the school's location.
claims.source_passage: Before 1994 there was little difference in overall school performance, no matter where the school was located.

claims.text: Post-1994 decentralization in Sweden led to wide differences in school performance and increased achievement gaps between rich and poor students.
claims.source_passage: Now there had developed wide differences in school performance, and wide gaps had opened between the performances of rich and poor students.

claims.text: The overall performance of Swedish students in PISA studies declined significantly following decentralization.
claims.source_passage: The overall performance of Swedish students in the PISA studies had declined significantly.

claims.text: Decentralization of the Swedish school system has not worked.
claims.source_passage: Decentralization, Professor Lewin concluded, had not worked.

claims.text: The 1994 Swedish curricula replaced detailed topic and time allocations with vague requirements that abandoned state control over knowledge content.
claims.source_passage: the previously detailed time allocations and topics were replaced by vague time allocations that required only a minimum guaranteed teaching time. Under the new curricula, the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school

claims.text: Swedish PISA verbal scores experienced the largest drop ever recorded by PISA between 2000 and 2012.
claims.source_passage: Between 2000 and 2012, Swedish PISA verbal scores fell from 516... to 483... It is the largest drop ever recorded by PISA.

claims.text: High equity and achievement in pre-1994 Sweden were the result of a deliberate national policy of 'equality-oriented centralism.'
claims.source_passage: Before 1994, high equity and achievement in Sweden had been no accident. It had been a chief aim of national policy... Sweden followed what he calls 'equality-oriented centralism'

claims.text: The Swedish principle of 'equivalence' replaced the common curriculum with the idea that education should be adapted to each pupil's individual circumstances.
claims.source_passage: A principle of 'equivalence' was legislated to replace the common curriculum... Education should be adapted to each pupil's circumstances and needs, based on the pupils' background, earlier experiences, language, and knowledge.

claims.text: The yardstick for educational 'equivalence' in progressive systems is the teaching of universal thinking skills rather than common content.
claims.source_passage: If equivalence doesn't require everyone to learn similar content, as before in Sweden, then what would be the yardstick for it? It is the answer given by progressive education since 1910. All pupils will be taught different, individually adapted topics-but all will be taught the same thinking skills

claims.text: The Swedish elementary curriculum became incoherent due to its new emphasis on individuality and thinking skills.
claims.source_passage: With the new emphasis on individuality and thinking skills, the elementary curriculum in Sweden became as incoherent as the curriculum had become in France and the United States.

claims.text: The destruction of the French and Swedish school systems was caused by an unwarranted confidence in progressive slogans regarding individualization and skills.
claims.source_passage: They were unlucky enough to believe the well-meant but misguided slogans about individualization and skills. Their past successes had given them an unwarranted confidence that they could get even better results by attending more closely to individual needs.

claims.text: Germany serves as an 'object lesson' in the opposite direction of Sweden, having improved its PISA standings.
claims.source_passage: Germany is an object lesson in the other direction. It has greatly improved its PISA standings

claims.text: France and Sweden destroyed effective school systems because they believed misguided slogans about individualization and skills.
claims.source_passage: Why did France and Sweden, which stood at the top in equity and achievement, suddenly destroy two of the most admirable and effective large school systems in the world? It was because they intended to make their good systems even better. They were unlucky enough to believe the well-meant but misguided slogans about individualization and skills.

claims.text: Sweden has deteriorated the most in social equality among PISA-studied nations, surpassed only by France.
claims.source_passage: Now in the PISA studies Sweden is second only to France as the country that has deteriorated the most in social equality.

claims.text: Germany significantly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity by adopting well-defined, specific curricula.
claims.source_passage: It has greatly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity. ... Reacting to the Schock, Germany began to adopt well-defined curricula. After that it began to score above average for developed nations.

claims.text: The key to Germany's academic improvement was making curriculum guides more aligned across states and more specific grade by grade.
claims.source_passage: the post-Schock curriculum guides put out by the Länder (equivalent to the states in the United States) are now much more aligned with each other, and are more specific grade by grade.

claims.text: Curricular specificity improves outcomes because teachers know exactly what to teach and students know what they must learn.
claims.source_passage: The teachers, he said, know specifically what to teach, and the students know what they have to learn.

claims.text: Ideological labels of left-versus-right are insufficient to explain educational declines in Sweden and France.
claims.source_passage: Sweden offers dramatic proof that left-versus-right ideological simplifications regarding the declines there and in France are not warranted.

claims.text: In Sweden, the movement toward individualism and skills in education originated from the political right.
claims.source_passage: In Sweden, the disastrous movement toward individualism and skills that destroyed its education came from the political right, not the left as in France.

claims.text: The educational positions of the Swedish political right and the French socialist left regarding individualization were functionally identical.
claims.source_passage: That statement from the right was exactly the position and almost the wording of the socialist left in France, which stated: 'The school shall take into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil.'

claims.text: The UK's Plowden Report (1967) institutionalized progressive, child-centered education as official left-wing doctrine in Britain.
claims.source_passage: Progressive education, long an undercurrent in teacher training in the United Kingdom, now began to be institutionalized as official left-wing doctrine. The Plowden Report encouraged schools to 'develop a more informal, child-centered style of education with an emphasis on individualization and learning by discovery'

claims.text: The Bennett Report (1976) indicated that progressive educational methods exacerbated inequalities without improving creativity.
claims.source_passage: The Bennett Report of 1976 argued that the data showed that the new methods were exacerbating inequalities without leading to any improvement in 'creativity.'

claims.text: Teacher-training institutions serve as 'theological institutes for progressivism' that indoctrinate union members.
claims.source_passage: Their membership has simply been indoctrinated by their teacher-training institutions, which are theological institutes for progressivism.

claims.text: Specific core curricula possess 'socially enabling power' because they narrow achievement gaps between groups.
claims.source_passage: In the United States, the independent-minded American Federation of Teachers has long supported the highly specific Core Knowledge Curriculum because of its socially enabling power, and its effectiveness in narrowing achievement gaps between groups.

claims.text: The American Federation of Teachers supports the Core Knowledge Curriculum because it narrows achievement gaps and provides socially enabling power.
claims.source_passage: the independent-minded American Federation of Teachers has long supported the highly specific Core Knowledge Curriculum because of its socially enabling power, and its effectiveness in narrowing achievement gaps between groups.

claims.text: The political left historically favored universalism—offering the same educational opportunities to all children in early years.
claims.source_passage: Ever since Condorcet and Jefferson, the left has favored universalism- that is, the idea of offering each child the very same educational opportunities in the early years.

claims.text: Educational individualism and the 'skills delusion' have no inherent logical connection to either the political left or right.
claims.source_passage: Educational individualism and the skills delusion have no inherent connection with either the left or right.

claims.text: The great weakness of Progressive Education is that it lacks a theory of social welfare, tending instead toward anarchy or extreme individualism.
claims.source_passage: The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare, unless it be that of anarchy or extreme individualism.

claims.text: Children from impoverished backgrounds require mastery of the intellectual tools of power rather than attempting to invent their own knowledge.
claims.source_passage: poor children needed to master the intellectual tools of power rather than implausibly trying to invent their own.

claims.text: Cognitive science establishes that broad, commonly shared knowledge is essential for effective language use.
claims.source_passage: cognitive science clearly indicates that broad, commonly shared knowledge is essential for effective language use.

claims.text: The 'common school' is a necessary institutional tool for ensuring equal opportunity in the face of modern specialization.
claims.source_passage: the 'common school' is necessary for 'reasons of equal opportunity,' as you put it, and to remedy the tendency of modernity and modernization to channel pupils/students into special(ized) schools meant to prepare them for technical, industrial, etc. jobs.

claims.text: Primary education (Grades PreK-5) must be depoliticized to focus on hard, specific, and common curricular content.
claims.source_passage: The early curriculum requires hard, specific thought, and commonality in grades preK-5. The depoliticizing of primary education could be the most important practical theme of this book.

claims.text: Individualism in early grade curricula disables children by depriving them of shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Individualism in the early grades disables many individuals. It is an error committed by both the left and the right.

claims.text: Educational individualism and curricular incoherence are the primary causes of the persistent achievement gaps in the US, Britain, France, and Sweden.
claims.source_passage: Individualism with its curricular incoherence has been a chief cause of stasis or worse in the effort to narrow the achievement gaps between racial, ethnic, and economic groups in the United States, Britain, France, and Sweden.

claims.text: Providential individualism is an outmoded intellectual residue that is not a necessary element of any political ideology.
claims.source_passage: Providential individualism is an outmoded residue, a sedimentary deposit from intellectual history. It is not a logical nor a necessary element in the aims of either left or right.

claims.text: By 1936, the United States already contained thousands of progressive public schools, a shift documented in the newsreel 'New Schools for Old'.
claims.source_passage: Already, by 1936, there were thousands of progressive public schools in the United States according to a March of Time newsreel of that year entitled "New Schools for Old."

claims.text: Between 2000 and 2012, Swedish student performance in PISA declined in math from 510 to 478 and in science from 512 to 485.
claims.source_passage: TABLE 7.1 Sweden PISA scores

claims.text: German PISA scores between 2000 and 2012 rose significantly, increasing from 490 to 514 in math and from 487 to 524 in science.
claims.source_passage: TABLE 7.2 German PISA scores

claims.text: The Jospin law established twenty-eight new education colleges across France's autonomous districts specifically to codify and teach progressive pedagogical theory.
claims.source_passage: The Jospin law also included the institution of twenty-eight new education colleges-one in each district and two extra ones in the district of Paris to train teachers in appropriate methods for the new system. They were to codify the profession of pedagogy in a scientific and orderly way (translation: they would teach progressive theory).

claims.text: The Swedish PISA verbal score decline moved the nation from 12 points above the United States in 2000 to 15 points below the United States by 2012.
claims.source_passage: Between 2000 and 2012, Swedish PISA verbal scores fell from 516, which was 12 points above the United States, to 483, which is 15 points below the United States

claims.text: The most important practical objective of the book 'Why Knowledge Matters' is the depoliticizing of primary education.
claims.source_passage: The depoliticizing of primary education could be the most important practical theme of this book.

claims.text: The theory of providential individualism is an outmoded intellectual residue or 'sedimentary deposit' from history rather than a logical requirement of political ideology.
claims.source_passage: Providential individualism is an outmoded residue, a sedimentary deposit from intellectual history. It is not a logical nor a necessary element in the aims of either left or right.

claims.text: The Swedish 1994 reform was predicated on the premise that teachers and principals are best positioned to design curricula because they possess superior knowledge of local conditions.
claims.source_passage: In contrast, the basic idea of 1994 law was that henceforth teachers and principals are considered to be in the best position to design the curriculum, because they best know the local conditions.

evidence.description: 2007 French Ministry of Education data compiled and published by Paola Mattei.
SAT verbal score trends showing a 0.5 standard deviation drop from 1960 to 1980.
Score drops on the Iowa Test of Educational Development and Minnesota College Aptitude Test (1965-1975) occurring during demographic stability.
ACT scores, California Achievement Test, and Stanford Achievement Test all showing drops of 1/4 to 1/3 standard deviation between 1965-1975.
The 1936 'March of Time' newsreel 'New Schools for Old' documenting the spread of progressive schools.
NAEP data showing flat verbal scores for 17-year-olds since 1971.
The 1980s French national curriculum, which specifically outlined discipline topics and time allocations per grade.
The commercial availability of Summer-study books and dictée practice materials in French bookstores based on the national curriculum.
A 1991 chart showing average scores for advantaged vs. disadvantaged ninth-grade students in France, Sweden, and Germany.
The 1989 loi Jospin, which mandated that schools respect home culture and individual characteristics rather than a common culture.
The 1989 legislative vote in the French National Assembly (280 in favor, 266 against).
The Bourdieu-Gros Report which provided the intellectual and structural framework for the Jospin Law.
The verbatim text of the preamble of the 1989 Jospin Law regarding student-elaborated curricula.
The establishment of twenty-eight new education colleges to train teachers in new methods.
Rising rates of primary students repeating grades due to inability to read or reckon.
Statistical disparities in university major selection correlated with social class in 1960s France.
Correlations between students' cultural tastes (music and movies) and their social class background.
Pierre Bourdieu's personal rise from a disadvantaged background where Standard French was not spoken at home.
Condorcet's 1790 plan 'A Common Education for Children' and Napoleon's meritocratic principles as the foundation for the French curriculum.
The French Ministry of Education's periodic 'soundings' (national longitudinal data) from 1987, 1997, and 2007 using identical test instruments and demographic categories.
Sounding results showing a decline of 0.4 standard deviations in literacy skills over two decades following the 1989 law.
Comparative data showing that students with better-educated parents suffered less decline than students from disadvantaged backgrounds under the new curriculum.
Figure 7.2: Longitudinal data comparing z-scores for French students in 1987 (knowledge-based curriculum) versus 2007 (skills-based curriculum).
Comparison of decline across social classes: Children of unemployed/laborers saw a drop of nearly 1.0 standard deviation, while children of executives saw a smaller decline.
Stability of French teacher entry standards and objective measures of teacher quality from 1987 to 2007.
The book by Rachel Boutonnet detailing the 'ideological conformity' in French teacher-training institutes.
Marc Le Bris's bestselling account describing the long-term failure of 'natural' reading methods and progressive pedagogy in France.
evidence.source_reference: Marc Le Bris, Your Children Won't Know How to Read or Count (2004)

evidence.description: An analysis by an association of French teachers and researchers (GRIP) identifying curriculum disintegration as the source of school failure.
evidence.source_reference: Groupe de Recherches Interdisciplinaires sur les Programmes (GRIP)

evidence.description: 2012 PISA international rankings showing France as having the most significant decline in educational equity.
François-Xavier Bellamy's 2014 book 'The Disinherited' which deconstructs the underlying ideas of French education.
Professor Leif Lewin's 2014 report commissioned by the Swedish government on the effects of decentralization.
PISA study data showing a significant decline in the overall performance of Swedish students post-1994.
PISA verbal score data showing a decline from 516 in 2000 to 483 in 2012.
Swedish Ministry of Education summary of the 1994 Education Act regarding 'equivalence.'
Comparison of Swedish PISA scores (deteriorating) vs. German PISA scores (improving).
Sweden's PISA scores from 2000 to 2012 showing a steady decline across reading, math, and science.
German PISA scores from 2000 to 2012 showing substantial improvement following the 'PISA Shock'.
Testimony from Manfred Prenzel, head of PISA studies in Germany, attributing success to aligned and specific curricula.
The Plowden Report (1967) which officially endorsed individualization and learning by discovery in the UK.
The Bennett Report (1976) which analyzed the impact of new educational methods on inequality and creativity.
The Bennett Report of 1976 showed progressive methods increased inequality without creative gains.
The American Federation of Teachers (AFT) long-standing support for Core Knowledge as an equity tool.
General findings from cognitive science regarding the necessity of shared background knowledge for language processing.
Joseph Buttigieg's scholarly interpretation of Antonio Gramsci's 'common school' as a humanistic and egalitarian necessity.
The historical performance and subsequent decline of educational equity in Sweden and France following curricular changes.

concepts.author_definition: A historical analogy for the French educational collapse following 1989, equating it to the military defeat of 1940 as a 'misunderstood tragedy' caused by avoidable intellectual errors.
A virus-like system of educational ideas prioritizing individualized, skill-based learning over shared content, which the author blames for the decline in literacy across multiple nations.
The 1983 federal report that officially recognized the decline in American educational performance.
The 1989 French educational law that decentralized the school system and replaced the common curriculum with localized 'projects' and individualized instruction.
The transition of the French school system from a high-achieving, equitable common-knowledge model to a lower-achieving progressive model, viewed as a natural experiment.
The French equivalent of American 'progressive education,' focusing on individual children, naturalistic learning, and the de-emphasis of rote memorization.
The national standards document created under the Jospin Law, characterized by the author as being too brief and nonspecific to guide instruction.
A derogatory term used by reformers to dismiss the systematic teaching of factual knowledge as a 'piling up of facts'.
A form of 'progressive education' that prioritizes the individual child, naturalistic development, and accommodates teaching to child characteristics over standardized content.
The theory that education systems are designed to perpetuate existing social stratifications and keep the underclasses submerged.

concepts.term: la méthode globale / la méthode mixte
concepts.author_definition: Naturalistic reading instruction methods that the author claims caused reading difficulties among French pupils.
Periodic national assessments of student competencies and demographics conducted by the French Ministry of Education.
concepts.source_passage: The French Ministry of Education takes periodic 'soundings' of the public school system, recording in detail the level of student competencies, along with demographic information about the students.

concepts.author_definition: The academic knowledge and vocabulary acquired in the home environment that allows students to succeed in the public sphere and on literacy tests.
The belief that education should focus on abstract, transferable skills rather than specific, cumulative knowledge content.
An all-purpose skill emphasized in French education colleges as the chief aim of instruction, prioritized over the transmission of information.
The breakdown of a coherent, nationwide curriculum into random subject matters, which Hirsch and GRIP argue prevents students from acquiring necessary common knowledge.
A 'quasi-whole-language' reading method that attempts to appease critics by occasionally mixing in phonics instruction but remains fundamentally naturalistic.
The redefined role of the teacher in progressive French pedagogy, acting as a 'guide on the side' rather than a direct instructor or lecturer.
The active passing on of a common cultural heritage and knowledge to the next generation, which is viewed by Hirsch as essential for equity.
concepts.common_alternative: Viewed by Bourdieu as a method of social reproduction that favors the 'inheritors' of elite culture.

concepts.author_definition: The shift of control over curriculum and knowledge content from a central state authority to local schools or market-driven choices.

A system where all children attend neighborhood schools and study the same subjects under a common national curriculum, with extra resources allocated to low-achieving students to ensure small differences in achievement between schools.

A legislative principle replacing common curriculum; it dictates that education should be of 'equivalent value' by adapting to individual needs and circumstances rather than by being the same everywhere.
concepts.common_alternative: Equity achieved through identical core content.

concepts.author_definition: The progressive educational focus on training students to think critically, solve problems, and communicate as a universal outcome that supposedly transcends the specific content being taught.
A national scandal and stock-taking in Germany triggered by the report of low PISA scores in 2000, which were below those of the US and the average of developed nations.
A pejorative characterization of teacher-training institutions, suggesting they prioritize ideological indoctrination in progressive theory over scientific or practical effectiveness.
The capacity of a shared, specific curriculum to empower marginalized students by providing the necessary knowledge to close achievement gaps.
The idea of offering every child exactly the same educational opportunities and content in the early years of schooling.
An approach that prioritizes personalization and individual child choice over a structured, common curriculum, often leading to curricular incoherence.
Gramsci's concept of a unitary school or common school of humanistic formation intended to create a 'general culture' before specialization.
The 1989 report, headed by Pierre Bourdieu, which provided the structural basis for the loi Jospin and advocated for a pluralistic, skills-based approach over the national curriculum.
A traditional French pedagogical exercise where students must write down spoken text with perfect spelling and punctuation, symbolizing the rigors of the old curriculum.
A term used by Roger Shattuck to describe the Wild Boy of Aveyron, adapted by Hirsch to describe the French abandonment of common knowledge as a large-scale natural experiment.
A 1967 British report that institutionalized progressive, child-centered, and individualized education as official left-wing doctrine in the UK.
The German states responsible for creating specific, grade-by-grade curriculum guides that led to Germany's PISA recovery.
Hirsch's metaphor for ideas like providential individualism that persist in educational policy as historical leftovers rather than through current logical necessity.

cases.name: France post-1989
cases.description: Following 1989 reforms, France adopted skill-based and individualized principles. This led to a collapse in achievement and equity, mirroring the US experience decades earlier. The author characterizes this as an avoidable intellectual disaster.
key_passages: The data showed another educational system that, like our own, had adopted skill-based, individualized, educational principles, and subsequently declined from being among the best and most equitable large school systems in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable.
The data showed that France had experienced a fully documented repetition of the US decline.

cases.name: United States 1960-1980
cases.description: A period where standardized test scores (SAT, ACT, etc.) plummeted across all student demographics following the widespread adoption of 1930s progressive curriculum reforms.
key_passages: SAT verbal scores had plummeted half a standard deviation in the twenty years between 1960 and 1980.
Ours began showing up in the 1960s. The origins of our decline can be clearly traced to our having made similar intellectual mistakes half a century before.

cases.name: Iowa and Minnesota (1965-1975)
cases.description: States with stable student demographics that still experienced significant test score drops, serving to disprove that the decline was caused by changing student populations.
key_passages: Particularly persuasive are the state tests in Iowa and Minnesota, administered to virtually all high school juniors and seniors in those states at a time when their pupil demographics were stable-thus eliminating demographic change as a cause of the declines in those states.

cases.name: France pre-1989
cases.description: A highly successful, centralized school system characterized by a common curriculum, specific grade-by-grade topics, and mandatory preschool (ecole maternelle). It achieved high equity and successfully integrated immigrant populations.
key_passages: In the 1980s, France could celebrate (with Sweden) the best large school systems in Europe.
The document allocated a specific number of minutes per week for the study of topics in each discipline in each grade.
It educated immigrants better than any other European nation.

cases.name: Germany (early 1990s)
cases.description: Cited as a poorly performing European school system due to its continued adherence to progressive educational ideas, contrasting with the success of the French model of that time.
key_passages: But note also the poor performance of Germany, still under the sway of progressive ideas.

cases.name: France 1977 (d'Estaing Era)
cases.description: A period characterized by the 'Common School' ideal where all French children were meant to attend the same schools and acquire the same knowledge to ensure social cohesion.
key_passages: to enable all French children to acquire the same knowledge in order to unify society and reduce inequality. Each child would go, d'Estaing said, to the "same" primary school and middle school.

cases.name: The Loi Jospin Reform (1989)
cases.description: The radical overhaul of the French school system that introduced decentralization, multiculturalism, and child-centered curricula, moving away from national uniformity.
key_passages: In 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school.
The very preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be "lockstep" any longer. It was going to be child centered.

cases.name: French teacher training schools (écoles normales) post-1960s
cases.description: Institutions where young teachers were 'indoctrinated' in progressive pedagogical theories, leading to a 'silent revolution' in how core subjects were taught despite a stable national curriculum.
key_passages: They had been instructed, during their training at the écoles normales, in a new point of view that of la nouvelle education, which we call "progressive education."
Gradually, progressive education infiltrated and then dominated teacher-training institutes in France.

cases.name: French university demographics in the 1960s
cases.description: A cohort studied by Bourdieu which included children of peasants and disadvantaged backgrounds, which the author interprets as proof of the system's success in enabling social mobility.
key_passages: What The Inheritors showed unwittingly was that the French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s had been doing an excellent job of bringing children of peasants to the university

cases.name: The 1989 French Reform
cases.description: The transformation of French education following the Bourdieu-Gros Report. It shifted the focus from content knowledge ('encyclopedism') to general skills, 'individuality,' and critical thinking. The author labels this 'American-style education' and blames it for the subsequent decline in student performance.
key_passages: Bourdieu-Gros Report recommended American-style education, and that is what the new law went on to require.
Its primary principle was that the goal of education to develop general skills and the 'personality' (i.e., the individuality) of the student.

cases.name: The French Experiment (1987-2007)
cases.description: The French school system shifted from a specific, cumulative common-core curriculum to an individualized, child-centered skills curriculum (Jospin Law). Over twenty years, despite constant teacher quality and demographics, literacy scores plummeted and the achievement gap between social classes widened significantly.
key_passages: The child-centered skills curriculum had caused the average of literacy scores to fall four-tenths of a standard deviation.
The French experiment... has pretty well proved that a specific, cumulative common-core curriculum... is far superior to the American-style individualized curriculum that followed it in France.

cases.name: Sweden post-1994
cases.description: Sweden decentralized its school system in 1994, incorporating free-market principles and individual choice. This reform replaced a highly egalitarian and high-performing system with one characterized by severe drops in literacy and numeracy and wide achievement gaps between rich and poor.
key_passages: In 2014, after a decade of the experiment, the Swedish government commissioned a report from Professor Leif Lewin regarding the effects of its decentralization of Swedish schools
He showed that decentralization had caused a severe drop in students' abilities in reading, writing, and arithmetic. It had greatly widened the achievement gaps between rich and poor students.

cases.name: Sweden's 1994 Reform
cases.description: Sweden abandoned its highly equitable common-core national curriculum in favor of a decentralized, individualized system. This included free choice of schools, market competition, and a shift in teacher roles from delivering state-mandated content to designing local curricula. The result was a dramatic decline in PISA scores and a significant increase in social inequality.
key_passages: the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school, and transferred that determination to local schools.
Sweden decided to abandon common subject matter in favor of personalized education and market competition.
It is the largest drop ever recorded by PISA.

cases.name: Sweden's decline (2000-2012)
cases.description: Sweden moved from being a top-tier educational system in achievement and equity to a state of significant deterioration. This was driven by a political-right movement favoring individualization and decentralized schooling, which aimed to adapt education to individual 'needs' but resulted in a sharp drop in PISA scores and social equality.
key_passages: In the PISA studies Sweden is second only to France as the country that has deteriorated the most in social equality.
In Sweden, the disastrous movement toward individualism and skills that destroyed its education came from the political right, not the left as in France.

cases.name: Germany's post-Schock recovery
cases.description: After a poor showing in the 2000 PISA tests, Germany underwent a national 'Schock' and restructured its education system. The Länder adopted aligned and grade-by-grade specific curricula. By 2012, Germany's scores in reading, math, and science had risen significantly above the average for developed nations.
key_passages: Germany is an object lesson in the other direction. It has greatly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity.
Reacting to the Schock, Germany began to adopt well-defined curricula. After that it began to score above average for developed nations.

cases.name: Britain 1976
cases.description: The release of the Bennett Report which provided evidence that progressive methods were failing and exacerbating inequality, leading to a major public and media backlash against the 'new methods.'
key_passages: The Bennett Report of 1976 argued that the data showed that the new methods were exacerbating inequalities without leading to any improvement in 'creativity.'
This led the Times of April 26, 1976, to issue a lead editorial entitled 'Progress Is Not Progressive.'

thinkers.name: Laurent Lafforgue
thinkers.key_idea: The 1989 French school reforms were an avoidable disaster ('débâcle') comparable to the military defeat of 1940.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts Lafforgue's historical analogy and uses his edited volume as a primary frame for understanding the French situation.
key_passages: His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989, just as higher-ups had made serious, avoidable military mistakes in 1940.

thinkers.name: Paola Mattei
thinkers.key_idea: Compiled and publicized the 2007 French Ministry of Education data that highlighted the system's decline.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses the data Mattei provided as the empirical foundation for his analysis of the French experiment.
key_passages: I first decided to write this book after I ran across the astonishing 2007 data compiled by the French Ministry of Education, recently placed on the Web by Paola Mattei of the University of Oxford.

thinkers.name: Lionel Jospin
thinkers.key_idea: Total change of the French education system to respect individual student and local characteristics.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch views Jospin as the architect of a 'catastrophic' and 'radical' shift away from successful common-school traditions.
key_passages: Lionel Jospin, the French Minister of Education, rose in France's National Assembly in June of 1989 to describe his government's plan for the total change of French education.

thinkers.name: Valéry Giscard d'Estaing
thinkers.key_idea: The goal of French schooling is to enable all children to acquire the same knowledge to unify society and reduce inequality.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites the former President to illustrate the long-standing French tradition of the 'Common School' that was abandoned in 1989.
key_passages: President Giscard d'Estaing in 1977, who stated the basic idea of French schooling: to enable all French children to acquire the same knowledge in order to unify society and reduce inequality.

thinkers.name: Pierre Bourdieu
thinkers.key_idea: The traditional national curriculum is non-egalitarian because it favors the culture of the wealthy and excludes others.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Bourdieu as the intellectual leader behind the reports that justified dismantling the common knowledge curriculum.
key_passages: Pierre Bourdieu had been chosen to head the committee... because he had been in the forefront of scholars who complained that the old-fashioned national curriculum was not as egalitarian as it claimed to be.

thinkers.name: Condorcet
thinkers.key_idea: A common education for children as a means to achieve equality.
thinkers.engagement: Cites him as the intellectual origin of the egalitarian, centralized French common curriculum.
key_passages: starting with Condorcet's plan of 1790, A Common Education for Children

thinkers.name: Bourdieu and Gros
thinkers.key_idea: The need for a total revolution of the French education system to overcome social stratification and make it more egalitarian.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues that their recommendations for individualization and localization actually caused the increase in social stratification they sought to fix.
key_passages: The Bourdieu-Gros Report demanded a total overhaul of the system to make it more egalitarian, and demanded nothing less than a revolution of the system.

thinkers.name: Roger Shattuck
thinkers.key_idea: The Wild Boy of Aveyron as a 'Forbidden Experiment' in human development.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Shattuck's concept of a 'forbidden experiment' as a metaphor for the French curriculum reforms, which he deems unethical due to their negative impact on children.
key_passages: My late friend Roger Shattuck wrote a book entitled The Forbidden Experiment about the Wild Boy of Aveyron... The French have now conducted a bigger natural experiment...

thinkers.name: Rachel Boutonnet
thinkers.key_idea: Exposing the ideological conformity and lack of intellectual substance in modern French teacher-training institutes.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites her journal/book as evidence of the 'progressive takeover' of the French teacher-training system.
key_passages: informed the public about the practices that had become prevalent in French teacher-training institutes... resolved to keep a journal.

thinkers.name: Marc Le Bris
thinkers.key_idea: The long-term failure of 'natural' progressive methods and the 'cultural catastrophe' of modern French schooling.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Le Bris to demonstrate that these failing methods have been dominant for decades and that parents are now forced to do the 'work of the school' at home.
key_passages: For twenty years, the National Education system has prevented me from doing my job.
My students, pampered by modern methods, have suffered educational disadvantages of which I am ashamed today.

thinkers.name: François-Xavier Bellamy
thinkers.key_idea: The urgency of transmission; deconstructing the ideas behind the decline of French education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Bellamy's book 'The Disinherited' as a brilliant critique of the current French educational crisis by a young teacher.
key_passages: a brilliant high school teacher still in his twenties published a well-received, probing, and beautifully written little book

thinkers.name: Leif Lewin
thinkers.key_idea: Decentralization of schools in Sweden failed to maintain equity or achievement.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Lewin's 2014 report to provide empirical evidence that decentralization and the abandonment of a national curriculum lead to educational failure.
key_passages: Professor Lewin concluded, had not worked.
Under the new curricula, the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school

thinkers.name: Professor Lewin
thinkers.key_idea: Decentralization of Swedish schools did not work and led to wide performance gaps.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes the Lewin Report extensively to provide an authoritative empirical basis for the claim that Swedish reforms failed.
key_passages: Decentralization, Professor Lewin concluded, had not worked.

thinkers.name: Professor Tomas Englund
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Englund's terminology to describe the successful pre-reform Swedish model.
key_passages: According to Professor Tomas Englund of Örebro University, Sweden followed what he calls 'equality-oriented centralism'

thinkers.name: Manfred Prenzel
thinkers.key_idea: The improvement in student performance is directly linked to curriculum guides that are aligned and grade-specific.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Prenzel to provide a causal explanation for the statistical success seen in Germany's PISA scores.
key_passages: When Manfred Prenzel... was asked to explain what caused German students to become so much better... he had a simple answer: the post-Schock curriculum guides... are now much more aligned with each other, and are more specific grade by grade.

thinkers.name: W. H. Auden
thinkers.key_idea: Skepticism toward 'permissiveness' (progressive education) as a successful educational strategy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Auden's poetic protest to illustrate the cultural and intellectual pushback against the Plowden Report's recommendations.
key_passages: Poet W. H. Auden, protesting, stuck his poetical oar in: 'Dare any call Permissiveness / An educational success?'

thinkers.name: Antonio Gramsci
thinkers.key_idea: Supported a universal common curriculum ('scuola unitaria') so poor children could master the intellectual tools of power.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Gramsci to prove that the principle of a common curriculum is not inherently conservative and is actually essential for leftist goals of social equity.
key_passages: He understood that poor children needed to master the intellectual tools of power rather than implausibly trying to invent their own.

thinkers.name: George Counts
thinkers.key_idea: Critiqued progressive education for failing to provide a theory of social welfare, leading to anarchy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Counts' 1932 address to the Progressive Education Association to show that even within the progressive movement, there was an early recognition of the dangers of individualism.
key_passages: The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare, unless it be that of anarchy or extreme individualism.

thinkers.name: Joseph Buttigieg
thinkers.key_idea: Interprets Gramsci's 'common school' as a democratic necessity for humanistic formation and equal opportunity.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Buttigieg's correspondence to validate his interpretation of Gramsci's educational philosophy against current progressive interpretations.
key_passages: for Gramsci the 'common school' is necessary for 'reasons of equal opportunity'

actors.name: French Ministry of Education
actors.context: Source of the 2007 achievement data showing the decline of the French system.

actors.name: Harvard Education Press
actors.context: Original publisher of the material included in the appendix.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: Minister of Education who introduced the 1989 reforms.

actors.name: Valéry Giscard d'Estaing
actors.context: Former President of France who advocated for common curriculum.

actors.name: Condorcet
actors.context: Historical proponent of the common curriculum in France since 1790.

actors.name: Valéry Giscard d'Estaing
actors.context: French President in 1977 who advocated for a unified school system with common knowledge.

actors.name: Jacques Derrida
actors.context: Member of the committee that produced the Bourdieu-Gros Report.

actors.name: The Left in the National Assembly (Socialists and Communists)
actors.context: The political majority that passed the Loi Jospin in 1989.

actors.name: French Teacher Unions
actors.context: Supported radical changes in 1989 because they believed the old curriculum was responsible for student failure.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: Author of the 'loi Jospin' which shifted France toward individualistic schooling.

actors.name: French Ministry of Education
actors.context: Responsible for conducting the 'soundings' that provided the data for Hirsch's analysis.

actors.name: Napoleon
actors.context: Established the meritocratic principle of 'a career open to talents' linked to the common school system.

actors.name: French Socialist Government
